title: Self-Regulatory Organizations (SROs)
focus: Regulatory guidelines, responsibilities, and governance structure of SROs.
rationale: SRO governance and responsibilities are highly testable as statement-based questions in UPSC prelims, particularly the specific numerical criteria for shareholding and independent directors.
sectionExcerpt: SROs must have adequate net worth and diversified shareholding, with no entity holding 10% or more of its paid-up share capital. They are responsible for framing codes of conduct, dispute resolution, ensuring regulatory compliance, and detecting early warning signals. Governance requires at least one-third of the Board of Directors, including the Chairperson, to be independent.
evidenceBullets: No entity shall hold 10% or more of an SRO's paid-up share capital.
Responsibilities include framing a code of conduct, dispute resolution, and ensuring regulatory compliance.
At least one-third of Board members, including the Chairperson, must be independent.

title: Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC)
focus: Core characteristics and regulatory distinctions of NBFCs compared to commercial banks.
rationale: The distinction between NBFCs and banks is a classic UPSC concept, particularly regarding demand deposits and payment systems.
sectionExcerpt: NBFCs are registered under the Companies Act, 1956, engaging in lending and investment while excluding agriculture, industry, and real estate activities. Unlike banks, NBFCs cannot accept demand deposits, are outside the payment and settlement system, and cannot issue cheques drawn on themselves.
evidenceBullets: Outside the payment and settlement system and cannot issue cheques on themselves.

title: Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) and Amalgamation
focus: Amalgamation phases, ownership structure, regulatory oversight, and statutory requirements of RRBs.
rationale: RRBs have a unique ownership structure and specific PSL/CRAR targets that are frequently tested in UPSC. The recent amalgamation news makes it highly relevant.
sectionExcerpt: The Central Government notified the amalgamation of 26 RRBs under the 'One State One RRB' principle, reducing the total to 28 RRBs. Established in 1975 on the Narasimham Working Group's recommendation, RRBs are jointly owned by the GoI (50%), State Government (15%), and a sponsoring commercial bank (35%). They are regulated by RBI, supervised by NABARD, and treated as cooperative societies for tax purposes. RRBs must allocate 75% of ANBC to Priority Sector Lending (PSL) and maintain a 9% CRAR.
evidenceBullets: Amalgamation reduces RRBs to 28 across 26 states and 2 UTs.
Established in 1975 based on Narasimham Working Group recommendations.
Ownership: GoI (50%), State Government (15%), Sponsoring Bank (35%).
Regulated by RBI and supervised by NABARD.
Treated as cooperative societies under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Must allocate 75% of ANBC to Priority Sector Lending (PSL).
Must maintain a Capital to Risk-Weighted Assets Ratio (CRAR) of 9%.
